coredata.dc:title: A novel type of fluorescence modulation mechanism of carbon dots derived from the competition of photoinduced electron transfer and fluorescence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lingqiao
Wei, Siqi
Zhang, Limei
Yin, Xinghang
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: A series of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are prepared under different solvent conditions from citric acid and different nitrogen sources, and a novel principle for modulating their fluorescence (FL) properties has been proposed. The nitrogen-doped carbon core can act as a fluorophore, causing diverse FL properties of N-CDs to a certain extent restricting electron delocalization. The solvent effect experiments show that the FL originates from the local emission (LE) of the carbon core. Significantly, the electron-rich substituents linked directly to the carbon core can act as electron donors, which lead N-CDs to undergo a normal “surface substituents-excited” photoinduced electron transfer (S-PET) or a nontypical “carbon core-excited” PET (C-PET) under alkaline or acidic conditions, respectively, causing an uncommon pH-sensitivity FL quenching of N-CDs. Since C-PET or S-PET dominated by pH could further regulate the surface charges of N-CDs, unseparated dual-channel specific recognition and the “AND” logic gate detection for Cu2+ and Chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH) in neutral conditions have been established.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence mechanism
pH-sensitive
Photoinduced electron transfer
Dual-channel recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of n-doping on optical properties
Charge-controlling recognition of Cu2+ and CPH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-CDs could be thought of as being made up of two parts (Donor-Acceptor system, D-A system) and the potential PET processes under different acid and base conditions.
Schematic diagram about the formation process of N-CDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra and emission spectra of N-CDs. The insets are images of the purified reaction products under UV light excitation.
Fluorescent QYs of SCDs-OH, UCDs-OH, FCDs-OH, SCDs-W, UCDs-W and FCDs-W.
XPS spectra of the synthesized SCDs-OH (A-a/C), UCDs-OH (A-b/D), FCDs-OH (A-c/E), SCDs-W(B-d/F), UCDs-W(B-e/G), FCDs-W(B-f/H).
The diagram of the effect of nitrogen content on emission wavelength and FL QYs of the N-CDs samples.

Plots of the normalized FL intensity (Purple ball) and emission peak position (Yellow ball) for (a) SCDs-OH, (b) SCDs-W at different pH values. Zeta potentials of (c) SCDs-OH, (d) SCDs-W at different pH values.

A possible structure model of pH-sensitive N-CDs and its schematic diagram of three luminescence states (the yellow LUMO and blue HOMO: orbital of the carbon core, the green HOMO and LUMO: orbital of the substituents).
Zeta potentials of the SCDs-OH, UCDs-OH, FCDs-OH, SCDs-W, UCDs-W, and FCDs-W.

3D FL spectra of (a) SCDs-W and SCDs-OH, (b) SCDs-W + Cu2+ and SCDs-OH + CPH, (c) SCDs-OH and SCDs-W + Cu2+, (d) SCDs-W and SCDs-OH + CPH without separation. (e) Schematic diagram about formation process of SCDs and “and” logic gate detection principle for Cu2+ and CPH.

head.ce:title: A novel type of fluorescence modulation mechanism of carbon dots derived from the competition of photoinduced electron transfer and fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lingqiao Zhang and Siqi Wei equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are prepared under different solvent conditions from citric acid and different nitrogen sources, and a novel principle for modulating their fluorescence (FL) properties has been proposed. The nitrogen-doped carbon core can act as a fluorophore, causing diverse FL properties of N-CDs to a certain extent restricting electron delocalization. The solvent effect experiments show that the FL originates from the local emission (LE) of the carbon core. Significantly, the electron-rich substituents linked directly to the carbon core can act as electron donors, which lead N-CDs to undergo a normal “surface substituents-excited” photoinduced electron transfer (S-PET) or a nontypical “carbon core-excited” PET (C-PET) under alkaline or acidic conditions, respectively, causing an uncommon pH-sensitivity FL quenching of N-CDs. Since C-PET or S-PET dominated by pH could further regulate the surface charges of N-CDs, unseparated dual-channel specific recognition and the “AND” logic gate detection for Cu2+ and Chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH) in neutral conditions have been established.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are defined as carbon nanomaterials with optical properties less than 10 nm in size. CDs are favored by researchers for their incomparable merits such as excellent photobleaching resistance, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and simple synthesis.[1–3] In addition, the properties of multi-color emission, long-wave luminescence and phosphorescence of CDs make them have great potential application value in the fields[4–8] of photocatalysis, sensor array, magnetic material and full-color display devices, all of which are attributed to the enhancement of FL quantum yields (QYs) of CDs and the in-depth study of FL modulation mechanism. As a powerful direction of surface modification, heteroatom doping can greatly improve the FL properties of CDs. The N atom doping plays an important role in the adjustment of QYs and luminescence by changing the internal electronic environment.[9,10] However, the effect of N atom doping on the optical and physicochemical properties of CDs is still in dispute, hence it is urgent to conduct in-depth research on it by simple synthesis means and systematic design scheme.

There are many methods and raw materials for CDs synthesis, which lead to the lack of a unified conclusion on the FL mechanism of CDs. At present, the main mechanisms are as follows: a. Related to the size of the carbon core and the degree of crystallization, mainly manifested as quantum size effect, which is derived from the limiting effect of π conjugation of C–C bonds on excitons in graphite-like structures.[11–13] b. Related to the surface defect state, which is often affected by surface modification, solvent action and heteroatom doping.[14–17] Massive previous studies believe that the FL properties of CDs are determined by their surface state. Due to the dangling bonds on the surface of oxygen-containing functional groups such as C–O, C=O, C–O–C, –COOH, the non-radiative transitions of CDs cause FL quenching. While surface modification can remove the dangling bonds and make the electrons return to the ground state in the form of radiation transition, resulting in enhanced FL[18,19] Actually, the complex FL mechanisms of CDs make the design and modulation of FL intensity and luminescence quite challenging. Therefore, the mechanism of regulating the optical performances of CDs through the synergistic effect of carbon core and surface state by chemical means still needs to be further investigated.

Fig. 2 illustrates the synthetic precursors and reaction conditions (solvents) of the six various types of fluorescent N-CDs studied (denoted as SCDs-OH, SCDs-W, UCDs-OH, UCDs-W, FCDs-OH, FCDs-W). To investigate the effects of different nitrogen sources and synthesis solvents on the optical properties of N-CDs, UV–Vis, spectra, FL excitation spectra, emission spectra and the optical images of the N-CDs samples were performed in Fig. 3
. The maximum absorption peaks of the six N-CDs are all located at 340 nm, while the shoulder peak near 400 nm appears in SCDs (Fig. 3 a, d) and the acromion at 480 nm exists only in FCDs-W (Fig. 3 f). The above results demonstrate that the difference in absorption peaks of N-CDs is not from the influence of the synthesis solvents, but primarily from the variation of nitrogen sources. Furthermore, under UV light excitation, N-CDs synthesized with NaOH as solvent all exhibit bright blue fluorescence, while N-CDs synthesized with water as solvent tend to emit green fluorescence, as illustrated in the insets in Fig. 3. When excited with respective optimal excitation wavelengths, the emission peaks of SCDs-OH, UCDs-OH, FCDs-OH, SCDs-W, UCDs-W, and FCDs-W were located at 449, 460, 470, 533, 512, 556 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength increased continuously, the emission wavelength of all N-CDs were redshifted, indicating that all N-CDs were excitation-dependent, see Figure S1 , which might be caused by the different surface states of N-CDs[20,21]. After several repeated measurements ( Table S1 ), the order of FL emission wavelengths of the six N-CDs remains constant.

The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of the six N-CDs were measured with quinine sulfate as the standard,[22] as shown in Fig. 4 . It is remarkable to note when employing the same carbon source and nitrogen source in different solvents, the QYs of the N-CDs synthesized with water as solvent are all higher correspondingly than that of N-CDs prepared with NaOH as solvent. Moreover, under the condition of the same carbon source and solvent, but different nitrogen sources, the order of QYs is exactly opposite to that of the emission wavelengths. The six N-CDs were divided into two groups taking the solvent as the standard, we noted that the longer the emission wavelength of the N-CDs, the lower their QYs in each group, which is consistent with the characteristics of the most CDs reported so far. The above results reveal that nitrogen sources and synthesis conditions (solvents) would not only affect the FL emission peaks of the as-synthesized CDs, but also play a decisive role in fluorescence QYs of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of n-doping on optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: In order to make certain the reason that causes the regular variation of optical properties, the morphologies of the six kinds of synthesized N-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Figure S2 , the N-CDs samples are demonstrating well-separated nanoparticles with average diameters of 5.98 nm (SCDs-OH), 3.78 nm (UCDs-OH), 1.55 nm (FCDs-OH), 5.71 nm (SCDs-W), 3.29 nm (UCDs-W), 1.18 nm (FCDs-W). It is worth noting that the particle sizes of the as-synthesized N-CDs are greatly affected by various nitrogen sources rather than the reaction solvents, which may be correlated to molecule structures of nitrogen sources. The sizes of the CDs prepared are the largest with SE as the precursor, followed by UA and FA are the smallest. Combined with the optical properties results, it is found that the FL emission wavelength of N-CDs does not redshift with the increase of size, indicating that the quantum size effect [11] has certain limitations on the regulation of optical performances of N-CDs in this study.

The surface functional groups of the six N-CDs were further detected by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer ( Figure S3 ). In FT-IR spectra, broad absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1, confirm the presence of –OH and –NH2 in the as-prepared N-CDs. Absorption bands peaked around 1739 and 1458 cm−1 are observed and attributed to –COOH and C–O stretching vibrations, which provide an explanation for the high water solubility and stability of all the N-CDs samples.[23] Moreover, the peaks at 2780, 1570, 1392, and 750 cm−1 are attributed to N–C=O, C=O, C–N, and C–H stretching vibrations. FT-IR spectra results demonstrate that the six N-CDs have the almost same functional groups. Besides, the surface groups were also investigated by XPS analysis (Fig. 5
A and 5B). C1s analyses in Figure S4
a-f reveal three types of carbon atoms: C=C/C–C (284.6 eV), C–N/C–O (285.6 eV), N–C=O/O=C–O (288.2 eV). The O1s spectra in Figure S4
g-l display two Gaussian peaks that can be fitted at around 532 eV (C=O) and 535.2 eV (C–O). The N1s spectra of the N-CDs can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.1 and 400.0 eV (Fig. 5), which are attributed to C–N–C and N–(C)3, confirming that the nitrogen element is successfully doped into the N-CDs samples.[24].

XPS characterization was further used to conduct elemental analysis on each N-CDs sample, and the results were shown in Table S2 . When employing NaOH as a solvent to synthesize N-CDs, the order of N element content is SCDs-OH > UCDs-OH > FCDs-OH, while the order is UCDs-W > SCDs-W > FCDs-W with H2O as solvent. Dramatically, in a corresponding solvent, the order of N content is perfectly relative to that of variation of optical properties. Combined with the spectral data of the N-CDs samples, it can be concluded that the larger amount of the N element content in N-CDs, the shorter the emission wavelength and the higher the QYs under the condition of the same reaction solvent and carbon source but various nitrogen sources (Fig. 6 ). Previous studies have confirmed by experimental observation and theoretical calculation that N atoms in the carbon-conjugated plane possess a strong electron-withdrawing ability.[25] Hence, it can be inferred that the blue shift of luminescence spectra of the synthesized N-CDs may be caused by certain restrictions on electron delocalization due to the introduction of the N element.[26,27] Besides, in our previous study, graphite N was considered to be an important factor for red fluorescence emission.[28] However, according to XPS analysis results, we note that the content of graphite nitrogen of the synthesized N-CDs was all relatively low, which may provide an explanation for the six N-CDs can but locate in the short-wavelength region. Therefore, this study well confirms that controlling the nitrogen doping amount of N-CDs by adjusting nitrogen sources and synthesis conditions (solvents) is a highly effective path to regulate the optical properties of fluorescent N-CDs.

There exist abundant hydrophilic groups such as –COOH, –NH2, and –OH on the surface of synthesized N-CDs, which can be used as hydrogen bond donors and hydrogen bond acceptors at the same time, therefore, N-CDs possess the potential capability of Excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT). [29,30] Taking SCDs-OH and SCDs-W as examples to investigate the changes of fluorescence spectra in different polar solvents, as shown in Figure S5 . The results show that the FL emission wavelength shifts blue with the increase of the dielectric constant of solvents, indicating that the FL emission of synthesized N-CDs originates from π*-n Local excited state (LE state), which may be because the rate of ESIPT emission is lower than that of FL emission.

Fig. 7 a, b and S6 , which follow, show that the FL emission wavelengths of the N-CDs have varied slightly with the change of the pH, indicating that synthesized N-CDs exhibit certain pH-sensitivity, which is in sharp contrast with the previously reported pH-insensitive CDs.[31] It is noteworthy that the changes of λem of N-CDs have been small, which further confirms that the FL emission of N-CDs does not originate from the ESIPT.[32] The weak response of λem to the pH is considered to be derived from the response of the graphitized nitrogen on the carbon core of N-CDs to the pH.[26,33,34] Moreover, with the increase of pH from 2 to 12, the FL emission peak positions of N-CDs show blue shift first and then red shift, and the turning point is still under neutral conditions. The results indicate that the surface functional groups of N-CDs present protonation/deprotonation at different pH values.[35–37].

In the meantime, the FL quantum yields of N-CDs also changed significantly with pH, and reached the maximum under neutral conditions. With the increase of acidity or alkalinity of the solution, the FL QYs both decreased, showing a remarkable pH dependence. Zeta potentials of N-CDs under different pH conditions were measured (Fig. 7 c, d and S7). In the range of pH 2∼11, Zeta potential decreased gradually from a high positive potential, approached zero under neutral conditions, and showed a high negative potential under alkaline conditions. Therefore, it could be inferred that the competition between PET and PL under acidic and alkaline conditions is a mechanism controlling the fluorescence properties of N-CDs.

The characterization data show that N-CDs are composed of carbon core with sp2 conjugated domain and edge substituents such as –NH2, –COOH, and –OH. Due to their varying electron-donating abilities, these substituents can be used as electron donors (D). Carbon cores possess sp2 conjugated domains and act as electron acceptors (A). [38–40] N-CDs can be considered as a D-A system with the potential to produce PET when the substituents are directly linked to the carbon core (Fig. 8 ). When the pH greater than 7, due to the effect of deprotonation (e.g. –COOH → –COO−, –OH → O−), the Zeta potentials on the surface of N-CDs show high negative potentials (Fig. 7 c, d), and the reduction potentials of D are improved. Normal PET from HOMO (n) of D (surface substituents) to HOMO of A (carbon core) can occur during photoexcitation, which is named Surface substituents-PET in this paper, S-PET for short. The generation of S-PET inhibits the FL emission of N-CDs, leading to the FL quenching of N-CDs in an alkaline environment. When the pH=7, –COO−, –OH, and –NH2 tend to form intramolecular hydrogen bonds, which disperse the negative charges into the hexatomic ring, which causes the charge density to reduce, making the Zeta potentials close to zero (Fig. 7 c, d), decreasing the reduction potentials of D and finally inhibit S-PET. In this case, N-CDs show normal LE emission with high FL quantum yields. When the pH less than 7, under the influence of protonation of surface substituent groups (e.g. –NH2 → –NH3 +, –OH → –OH2 +), the Zeta potentials of N-CDs are highly positive (Fig. 7 c, d), and the surface substituents have the LUMO that can accept electrons. The abnormal PET of the LUMO of the carbon core (A) to the substituent groups (D) is prone to occur during photoexcitation (named Carbon core-PET; C-PET), resulting in FL quenching of N-CDs in an acidic environment.

The relationship between Zeta potential (Fig. 9 ) and FL of six kinds of N-CDs samples was further analyzed. N-CDs synthesized by the same carbon source and nitrogen source under neutral and alkaline conditions have high positive potentials and high negative potentials, respectively, and both can experience abnormal C-PET and normal S-PET. The experimental results (Fig. 4) showed that the FL quantum yields of N-CDs synthesized by the same carbon source and nitrogen source under neutral conditions were higher than those under alkaline conditions, indicating that normal S-PET was more likely to occur and the inhibition of FL was more obvious.

Based on the above analysis, a novel model has been proposed to demonstrate the competition between normal or abnormal PET and FL of N-CDs in different acid-base environments, aiming to further regulate purposefully FL emission of CDs by controlling the D charge, which provides a meaningful new idea for the design of fluorescence “on/off” of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge-controlling recognition of Cu2+ and CPH
ce:section.ce:para: Copper, as one of the essential trace elements in animals and plants, plays an important role in maintaining the normal physiological function of the human body.[41,42] The presence of appropriate Cu2+ in the human body is beneficial to maintain the normal functions of the body. Chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH) is a phenothiazine medicine containing aliphatic side chains and is used in the treatment of diseases (e.g. depression, schizophrenia and mania), as well as with hypotensive, antiemetic and other effects.[43,44] Detecting the agent is quite important for monitoring and obtaining optimal treatment concentrations for patients. Therefore, the development of a rapid, accurate and effective method to determine Cu2+ and CPH in body fluids is significant for medical diagnosis.

Firstly, the responses of the fluorescence spectra of the six N-CDs to the metal ions were performed. 17 kinds of common metal ions (Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Na+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+) were selected for the experiment, and results showed among the six N-CDs, the fluorescence spectrum of SCDs-W is only responsive to Cu2+, but hardly respond to other metal ions ( Figure S8 ). Moreover, five other N-CDs fluorescence spectra show little response to the 17 metal ions. Hence, it demonstrates that Cu2+ has a certain selective quenching effect on SCDs-W, and the fluorescence lifetime detection results show that the quenching could be attributed to static quenching ( Figure S9 ), which provides a new method for environmental analysis and detection of Cu2+. Herein, a fluorescence signal display in Table S3 and Figure S10 of the N-CDs samples to metal ions sensing was plotted, which illustrates that SCDs-W has a specific response to Cu2+. It can be seen from Table S3 that the charge on the surface of N-CDs is positive or negative, which is not the reason for a specific selective response of the N-CDs to Cu2+, the combination of SCDs-W and Cu2+ may largely root in the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs.

In our previous work, we found that SCDs-OH could be used as a probe for the detection of chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH) with high sensitivity and selectivity, and suitable for CPH detection in body fluid samples.[45] Therefore, When adopting SE as a nitrogen resource precursor, the synthesized N-CDs with water as solvent could be applied as fluorescent probes for recognizing Cu2+, while the synthesized N-CDs with NaOH as solvent could be applied as fluorescent probes for recognizing CPH. It well confirmed that the two SCDs with opposite charges, which were synthesized from the same precursor show specific responses to Cu2+ and CPH respectively.

According to the selective quenching responses of SCDs (prepared from CA and SE) for Cu2+ and CPH, a new method for dual-channel detection of Cu2+ and CPH without separation has been developed. Fig. 10
a shows the 3D fluorescence spectra of SCDs-W and SCDs-OH without adding fluorescence quenching agents. It can be clearly seen that there are two emission centers located at around 440 and 530 nm, respectively. Besides, it is found that once the mixture is added the quenching agents Cu2+ and CPH are simultaneous, the intensities of both emission peaks decrease sharply, and the quenching degree both reach more than 99% (Fig. 10 b). However, if Cu2+ or CPH is just added, there is only one of the fluorescence peaks disappears and another peak remains, as shown in Fig. 10 c, d. Consequently, the dual-channel detection of Cu2+ and CPH in a system without separation has been established.

Herein, an “AND” logic gate was established ( Table S4 ), where Cu2+, CPH, SCDs, and surface charges of N-CDs were selected as input signals, and the changes of N-CDs fluorescence signals were applied as the output signal. The existence of the above signals was defined as 1, and the non-existence was defined as 0. It was found that only when Cu2+, SCDs-W, positive charge and CPH, SCDs-OH, negative charge input, the output signal was 1, and the rest were 0. Hence, the “AND” logical gate requires three conditions to be activated simultaneously, as illustrated in Fig. 10 e.

In summary, we report herein a novel type of FL modulation mechanism by systematically studying the six N-CDs with various optical properties. N-CDs can be regarded as a D-A system where the charge of D can be controlled by changing the pH and changing its oxidation–reduction potential, leading to the generation of normal S-PET or abnormal C-PET. The competition of PET and FL can produce effectively controlled modulation of optical properties of N-CDs. These data have important significance for understanding the fluorescence modulation mechanism of N-CDs. Finally, an interesting unseparated two-channel recognition, as well as “AND” logic gate operation of Cu2+ and CPH by charge control in a neutral environment has been established.

Materials: Citric acid and NaOH were from Kelon (Chengdu, China), Semicarbazide, Urea and formamide were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used as received without any purification. Ultrapure (Milli-Q) water was used for all experiments.

Characterizations: The N-CDs were synthesized by microwave heating (Guangdong, China). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The PL spectra were measured on a F-2500 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). TEM images of N-CDs were carried out by transmission electron microscope (FEI, America) with a working voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectra were collected on a 8400S (Kyoto, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using a K-Alpha (Plus) (Thermo Scientific, Hitachi, Japan). Zeta potentiometric analysis was carried out on a NanoBrook Omni20502 (Bruker, Hong Kong).

The preparation of N-CDs: CA (1.0 g) and SE (0.50 g) were dissolved in NaOH 10 mL (0.3 M) and H2O (10 mL), ultrasound for 10 min. Then put it in the microwave oven and heat it for 90 s. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the resulting solid powder was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water and filtered through a 0.20 μm microporous filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Then put the solution in dialysis tubes (MWCO: 500 Da) for dialysis for 12 h in order to remove small molecules, and SCDs-OH and SCDs-W were obtained respectively. Finally, the purified solution was stored in the refrigerator at 4℃. Then SE was changed into UA and FA, respectively. The other reaction conditions and treatment methods were the same as the above method. The solution reacted with CA to obtain UCDs-OH, UCDs-W, and FCDs-OH, FCDs-W, respectively.

Detection of Cu2+ and CPH: 20 µL N-CDs, 50 µL Britton-Robinson buffered solution (pH=7.0), 200 µL10.0 mmol/L Cu2+ or 10.0 mmol/L other metal ions or 100.0 µmol/L CPH were added into 2 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, dilute with ultrapure water to the scale and mix intensively. After reacting at room temperature for 10 min, measure the corresponding fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

QY Measurements: Take Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (Φs = 54.2%) as reference, the calculation is as follows:
Φ u = Φ s
F U
F S
A S
A U

Where Φu is the fluorescent quantum yield of N-CDs, Fu and Fs are the fluorescence integral area of N-CDs and Quinine sulfate respectively, As and Au are the absorbance of Quinine sulfate and N-CDs respectively.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123276.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-Enzymatic-Browning-Reaction: A Versatile Route for Production of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots with Tunable Multicolor Luminescent Display
